macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Order of the Nevi'im (Prophetic Books)

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to the order of Tanakh scrolls
Sidran shel nevi'im: the arrangement of the prophetic books and why Hoshea comes first among the Later Prophets
Discussion of why Hoshea is grouped with the Trei Asar rather than appearing separately as the first of the Later Prophets
The chronological arrangement of Yeshayahu, Yirmiyahu, and Yechezkel by thematic continuity rather than strict chronology

macro_segments.title: The Order of the Ketuvim (Writings) and the Significance of Order for Compilation

micro_segments.title: Sidran shel Ketuvim: the arrangement of the Writings as presented in the Gemara, with Rus, Tehillim, Iyov, Mishlei, Koheles, Shir HaShirim, Kinos, Daniel, Megillat Esther, Ezra, and Divrei HaYamim
Explanation of why we don't begin with suffering and the symbolic progression through Shlomo's books
Tosafot's explanation of the placement of Iyov between Tehillim and Mishlei due to shared tropal system and genre

macro_segments.title: Authorship of the Biblical Books: Mi Katvan (Who Wrote Them)

micro_segments.title: Moshe wrote the Torah and the portion of Bilam; Yehoshua wrote his own book and the last eight pesukim of the Torah
Yirmiyahu wrote his own book and the book of Melachim, connecting the destruction narratives
Chizkiyahu and his group compiled and edited Yeshayahu, Mishlei, Shir HaShirim, and Koheles after the deaths of their authors
Anshei Knesset HaGedolah (members of the Great Assembly) wrote or canonized Yechezkel, Trei Asar, Daniel, Megillat Esther, and finalized Ezra-Nechemia

macro_segments.title: Authorship of the Biblical Books: Mi Katvan (Who Wrote Them) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara debate on whether Moshe could have written the account of his own death and the model of direct divine dictation
Shmuel wrote his book and Shoftim; the final eight pesukim involve questions of authorship similar to the Torah
David wrote Sefer Tehillim through ten elders, with specific perakim attributed to named authors

macro_segments.title: The Last Eight Pesukim of the Torah: Direct Dictation, Moshe's Tears, and the Word-for-Word Model

micro_segments.title: Tanna's debate: can Moshe have written about his own death, and the model of direct divine dictation (word for word) versus inspired prophecy
Second Tanna's interpretation: God dictates while Moshe writes with tears, establishing the concept of Torah min Hashamayim as verbatim dictation
Significance of the model of direct dictation for understanding Torah min Hashamayim and its contrast with inspired authorship
The Gra's mystical interpretation: Moshe wrote in invisible tears and Yehoshua crystallized the letters after Moshe's death
The special status of the last eight pesukim as requiring a single reader (yachid kore otan) and Rashi's explanation versus Tosafot's alternative

macro_segments.title: The Book of Iyov: Its Existence, Authorship, Historical Dating, and Universal Relevance

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Levi bar Lachma argues Iyov lived in the days of Moshe, supported by the phrase 'yichtevun milai' and Moshe as mechokek (engraver)
Rava's alternative: Iyov lived in the days of the meraglim (spies), connecting the narrative of uncertainty and faith during the wilderness period
Anonymous opinion that Iyov was a mashal (parable) rather than a historical narrative; this opinion is rejected by Rabbi Shmuel bar Nachmani
Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Eliezer: Iyov was among those who returned at the time of the Second Temple, with his school in Tiberias
Ashi's calculation: Iyov lived 210 years, parallel to the duration of Jewish slavery in Egypt, framing his suffering within that historical context
Gemara's resolution that Iyov lived the length of time that Bnei Yisrael were in Egypt, but not necessarily at that historical moment
Citation of Iyov, Elifaz, Bildad, Tzofar, and Elihu as prophets to the nations of the world, raising the question of whether Iyov was Jewish or non-Jewish
Gemara's conclusion that the primary prophecy of Iyov was directed to the non-Jewish nations, making it fundamentally universal unlike the prophets to Israel
